ORGANIZATION: University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee Center for Information Policy Research
DESCRIPTION OF REQUEST: The University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee Center for Information Policy Research (http://cipr.uwm.edu) is researching the use of "cloud," web-based, or third-party computer services by public libraries across the country. The Chicago Public Library has been identified as a customer of the BiblioCommons cloud-computing platform, delivering an enhanced and interactive online catalog for your patrons. The project's goal is to analyze how public libraries utilize cloud computing services, as well as how they address and communicate issues that might impact patron privacy. The results of the research will include a summary report of findings, and the development of a set of best practices to guide future implementations of cloud computing in public settings, with the goal of finding a suitable balance between need to provide cost-efective technology-based services while also protecting patron privacy. Your participation in this project will help to ensure that our results provide a complete understanding, as well as useful guidance, for helping us navigate this increasingly important issue. On behalf of the UW-Milwaukee Center for Information Policy Research, and in accordance with the Illinois Freedom of Information Act, 5 ILCS 140, I request copies of the following information: 1. All contracts, agreements, or related legal/vendor documents the Library might have with BiblioCommons, its partners, or representatives. 2. All internal policies, documented procedures, or other materials related to the initial installation and continued implementation of BiblioCommons products and services. 3. All notices provided to patrons related to the Library's collection and use of patron data, including the Library's privacy policy (if one exists), user/patron agreements, or related materials. Please send copies to: Dr. Michael Zimmer; UW-Milwaaukee Center for Information Policy Research; Northwest Quadrant Building B, 3rd Floor; 2025 E. Newport Ave.; Milwaukee, WI 53211. Or by email to: zimmerm@uwm.edu. I look forward to hearing from you in writing within five working days, as required by the Act 5 ILCS 140(3). If you expect a significant delay in responding to this request, please contact with information about when I might expect the requested records. Please also inform me if there are any fees for searching or copying these records. Thank you for your consideration.

Hello all, 

Thank you for your e-mail. I am in receipt of it. This is Michael A. Ayele sending this message though I now go by W. You may call me W. I am writing this letter in response to your latest correspondence, which I have read through. I am contacting you to file an appeal to the records request I had submitted with your offices dealing with the requirements of the Jeanne Ann Clery Disclosure of Campus Security Policy and Campus Crime Statistics Act . Please be advised that I have concerns about your response.  Specifically, I am concerned about your failure to promptly disclose records detailing (1) the undergraduate, graduate and law schools you have previously attended and visited; (2) the time, the place and the manner in which you were first informed about the rape and murder of Jeanne Ann Clery. As a representative of the media and a member of the general public, I am worried about the manner in which I have come to be informed about the requirements of the Jeanne Ann Clery Disclosure of Campus Security Policy and Campus Crime Statistics Act.  In hindsight, I am also worried about the manner in which I have come to be informed about the brutal rape and murder of Jeanne Ann Clery on April 1986. As a representative of the media and a member of the general public, I cannot recall any incident of rape and murder, which has had a more profound and devastating impact on American society and post-secondary education than that of Jeanne Ann Clery in the past 40 years. As a representative of the media and a member of the general public, I feel comfortable asserting that not much is known about Jeanne Ann Clery except what is told about her through others. Specifically, not much is known about what she thought of issues dealing with chauvinism, discrimination, misogyny, racism, and sexism. Please be advised that I have concerns about several reports, which mention the requirements of the Jeanne Ann Clery Disclosure of Campus Security Policy and Campus Crime Statistics Act without referencing a paper written by Jeanne Ann Clery in high school and in her freshman year at Lehigh University. When I say: “I really don’t know Jeanne Ann Clery,” I mean that literally and figuratively. I have never met Jeanne Ann Clery: she died before I was born. I really don’t know what Jeanne Ann Clery would have thought of Donald Trump being invited at the graduation ceremony of Lehigh University Class of 1988 two years following her brutal rape and murder. I really don’t know what Jeanne Ann Clery would have thought about the decision of Lehigh University to extend an invitation to a man, who had not been respectful of affirmative and effective consent in his sexual relationships with women. I really don’t know what Jeanne Ann Clery would have thought of statements he made that have gone viral dealing with “grabbing women by the p****.” [i] I really don’t know what Jeanne Ann Clery would have thought about any man who would advocate for the death penalty in the State of New York, while treating women the way Donald Trump has. As a representative of the media and a member of the general public, I really can’t say “she would have approved of this,” and/or “she would have disapproved of that,” because of the lack of literature dealing with what she actually believed. As a representative of the media and a member of the general public, I really don’t know what you think of this as well. 

As a representative of the media and a member of the general public, I am worried about several colleges and universities I have previously attended and visited failing to mention the invitation that was extended to Donald Trump by Lehigh University in Fiscal Year 1988 on their websites and in their ASR. As a representative of the media and a member of the general public, I have concerns about Donald Trump being invited to speak at the graduation ceremony of Lehigh University Class of 1988 two years following the brutal rape and murder of Jeanne Ann Clery. As a representative of the media and a member of the general public, I find it problematic that someone who has not been respectful of affirmative and effective consent in his sexual relationships with women would in the State of New York advocate for the death penalty. I believe it to be troublesome. As a representative of the media and a member of the general public, I do believe college and university students of the USA should have the right to know that (1) Donald Trump was invited to speak at the graduation ceremony of Lehigh University Class of 1988 two years following the brutal death of Jeanne Ann Clery; (2) several credible news stories from reputable sources have indicated that Donald Trump has not been respectful of affirmative and effective consent in his sexual relationships with women; (3) Donald Trump has coerced the women he was not respectful of affirmative and effective consent to sign Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDA); (4) Lehigh University has recently rescinded the honorary degree awarded to Donald Trump in 1988 for unclear reason; (5) Donald Trump has advocated for the death penalty in the State of New York in the late 1980s; (6) the Department of Justice (DOJ) has resumed federal executions during his administration.  {US Government to Resume Executions After Nearly 2 Decades | Voice of America - English (voanews.com) } {US Carries Out 2nd Federal Execution This Week | Voice of America - English (voanews.com)} {US Executes First Woman Since 1953 | Voice of America - English (voanews.com)}  
W (AACL)
Anti-Racist Human Rights Activist
Audio-Visual Media Analyst
Anti-Propaganda Journalist 

P.S: As a representative of the media and a member of the general public, I hope you will contact your alma mater and ask questions about why you were not informed about (1)  the invitation Donald Trump was extended by Lehigh University in Fiscal Year 1988 two years following the brutal death of Jeanne Ann Clery; (2) the honorary Degree he was bestowed in Fiscal Year 1988 two years following the brutal death and murder of Jeanne Ann Clery; (3) his failure to be respectful of affirmative and effective consent in his sexual relationships with women; (4) the NDA he had women with whom he was not respectful of affirmative and effective consent sign and; (5) his loud advocacy in favor of the death penalty in the state of New York in the late 1980s (if you have previously not been so).  I hope you will have in this Black History Month of Fiscal Year 2021 a conversation with your alma mater of events which were shared with you in the History classes you may have taken as a college/university student. I hope you will also have a discussion about the stories you were not.

I am aware that while the law allows your department to withhold specific categories of exempted information, you are required by law to release any segregable portions that are left after the exempted material has been deleted or coded from that data that I'm seeking. An inability to provide all the information within the time frame allotted by the law should not prevent the release of readily available documents or files.

The records request is not for commercial use and is in the public interest because the information will reveal the utilization and the impact of the Summer Learning Program on the children by different library branches.

Thank you so much. 

Best regards,
